meeting__item_details.title_of_item: Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet

submission__presentation.submitted_by: Michael M. Anderson, Public Works Director
submission__presentation.presenter_name_title: Michael M. Anderson, Public Works Director
submission__presentation.estimated_time_needed: 15 minutes – staff presentation (8–10 minutes) plus Council questions (5–7 minutes)

issue_details.summary_of_issue:
The Public Works Department is requesting authorization from the Brainerd City Council to purchase one (1) replacement tandem-axle snowplow truck to replace Unit #24, a 2008 International plow truck that has reached the end of its useful life. Unit #24 currently has over 165,000 miles and approximately 11,400 engine hours, with significant corrosion to the frame, dump box, and underbody plow components due to extensive exposure to winter road salt. Over the past three winter seasons, maintenance and repair costs on this unit have increased considerably, including frequent hydraulic line failures, brake system repairs, and electrical issues related to corrosion.

In the 2025–2026 winter maintenance plan, the City’s level-of-service expectations for snow and ice control on priority routes require that we have at least six reliable front-line plow trucks available at all times. With Unit #24 increasingly out of service for repairs, staff has had to rely on overtime use of smaller backup trucks and contracted assistance during larger storm events. This has resulted in slower clearing times on residential routes and increased complaints from residents, particularly during back-to-back storm events.

The proposed replacement truck is a 2026 Freightliner 108SD chassis with a Henderson stainless steel dump body, front plow, underbody scraper, and stainless steel V-box spreader with pre-wet system. The stainless steel components are expected to significantly extend the service life of the equipment and reduce corrosion-related repairs. The truck would be purchased through the Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV) program, which the City has historically used to obtain competitive, compliant pricing on heavy equipment.

The current lead time for ordering and delivery of a new plow truck is approximately 10–12 months. In order to ensure the replacement unit is available prior to the 2026–2027 winter season, the order needs to be placed in early 2026. The Streets Division and Finance Department have reviewed this purchase in relation to the approved 2026 Capital Improvement Plan (CIP), and this replacement is consistent with the City’s fleet replacement schedule adopted during the 2025 budget process.
issue_details.alternatives_options_effects_on_others_c:
ALTERNATIVES CONSIDERED:

1. Defer replacement for one to two years and continue operating Unit #24
   - Staff considered keeping Unit #24 in service for an additional one to two winter seasons. However, based on the current condition of the frame, dump body, and hydraulic systems, both the Fleet Maintenance Supervisor and our outside mechanic have expressed concerns about continued reliability and safety. The truck has already experienced two in-season breakdowns while plowing primary routes during the 2024–2025 winter, which resulted in delayed clearing and required other routes to be covered by smaller trucks not designed for heavy accumulation.
   - Deferring replacement would likely increase annual repair costs. Over the last 24 months, we have spent approximately $18,750 on major repairs to Unit #24, compared to an average of $6,000–$8,000 annually in prior years. We anticipate that trend to continue or worsen, particularly as corrosion advances. There is also an increased risk of catastrophic failure (e.g., frame crack, major hydraulic system failure) that could take the unit out of service permanently in mid-season.

2. Purchase a used plow truck instead of a new unit
   - Staff reviewed used heavy-duty plow truck listings through several municipal surplus sites and regional dealers. While initial purchase prices for used units appear lower, most available used trucks in our region are of similar age and mileage to our existing fleet, and many show visible corrosion on frames and boxes. Purchasing a used unit would likely provide only a short-term solution and could result in higher life-cycle costs due to earlier major repairs and shorter remaining service life.
   - In addition, it is difficult to confirm the full maintenance history and prior usage of used units, and they often do not come equipped with stainless steel components or pre-wet systems, which are important for our long-term corrosion control and salt reduction goals.

3. Contracting additional private snowplowing services instead of replacing the truck
   - The City has occasionally used private contractors on a limited basis for cul-de-sacs, alleys, and overflow work during large storm events. While contracting out some work can be helpful as a supplemental tool, relying on contractors as a primary replacement for a core plow truck is not ideal. Contractors are not always available when needed, may not be familiar with our detailed route priorities, and do not always have equipment configured for salt and brine application consistent with our standards.
   - Contracting out an equivalent amount of plowing capacity to replace a tandem-axle truck would likely exceed the annualized cost of ownership over the life of a new unit, especially when considering the need for consistent, reliable coverage of our arterial and collector streets.

EFFECTS ON OTHERS:

- Residents and Businesses: Timely snow and ice control improves roadway safety, reduces the risk of crashes, and helps ensure residents can get to work, school, and medical appointments. During the January 2025 storm, for example, we received 27 calls from residents on the northeast side of the city noting delayed plowing compared to prior years. Having a reliable replacement for Unit #24 will help us restore and maintain our target plowing timeframes.

- Public Works Staff: Continued operation of a deteriorating truck increases the risk of in-service breakdowns and can create stressful working conditions for operators, particularly during overnight storms. A new, more reliable unit will reduce operator fatigue associated with managing equipment issues in the field and allow staff to focus on safe and efficient plowing.

- Environmental Impact: The proposed truck includes a pre-wet system and modern spreader controls, allowing for more precise application of salt and brine. This supports the City’s ongoing efforts to reduce overall salt usage and minimize chloride loading into area lakes and streams, consistent with MPCA guidance.

COMMENTS:

Staff has coordinated with the Finance Department to confirm that this purchase is consistent with the 2026 CIP and the equipment replacement schedule reviewed during the most recent budget process. We have also reviewed similar equipment specifications and pricing from neighboring cities (Baxter, Little Falls, and Crosby) to confirm that the proposed configuration and cost are in line with current market conditions. The Streets Division operators who will use the truck were included in the specification review to ensure the equipment meets operational needs and is compatible with our existing maintenance practices.
issue_details.recommended_action_motion: Motion to authorize the purchase of one (1) 2026 Freightliner 108SD tandem-axle snowplow truck with Henderson stainless steel dump body, front plow, underbody scraper, and stainless steel V-box spreader with pre-wet system, from Boyer Trucks (St. Cloud) through the Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Venture (Contract No. XYZ-2026-PLW) in the amount of $298,450.00, including tax and estimated delivery fees; and to adopt the attached resolution designating this purchase as a 2026 Capital Improvement Plan expenditure to be funded from the Public Works Equipment Replacement Fund (Fund 402).

financial_impact.please_explain:
This purchase is included in the adopted 2026 Capital Improvement Plan (CIP) under the Public Works – Streets Division equipment replacement schedule. The total estimated cost of $298,450.00 includes the chassis, dump body, plow equipment, stainless steel spreader with pre-wet system, dealer setup, Minnesota sales tax, and estimated delivery charges. The base contract price through the Minnesota State Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV) program for the specified configuration is $276,900.00. After applying the current state contract discounts and adding required options (LED lighting package, additional safety cameras, and underbody scraper upgrades), the subtotal comes to approximately $284,700.00. With sales tax and delivery, the total project cost is estimated at $298,450.00.

Funding for this purchase will come from the Public Works Equipment Replacement Fund (Fund 402), which is supported through annual transfers from the General Fund and prior-year equipment reserve balances. During the 2025 budget process, the Council approved a planned allocation of $300,000.00 in Fund 402 for the replacement of one tandem-axle plow truck in 2026. As of the most recent Finance Department report (November 2025), the projected year-end balance in Fund 402 is sufficient to cover this purchase without requiring any additional levy increase or interfund loan.

No bonding or external financing is anticipated for this purchase. The replacement of Unit #24 is part of the City’s long-term fleet management strategy, which aims to replace front-line plow trucks on a 15–18 year cycle to avoid excessive maintenance costs and unplanned capital needs. By purchasing this truck now, we expect to reduce ongoing repair expenses on the existing unit and avoid emergency replacement scenarios.

There will be a modest increase in annual insurance premiums associated with insuring a newer, higher-value vehicle; however, this increase is expected to be offset by reduced repair expenditures. Fuel usage is expected to be similar or slightly improved due to more efficient engine and hydraulic systems. Overall, the long-term financial impact is expected to be neutral to slightly positive when considering reduced maintenance costs, improved reliability, and the avoidance of emergency contractor expenses during major storm events.
